column7: message

The Japanese Americans were held in camps and  nearly exterminated in the U.S., faced widespread racism, are required to get higher SAT scores to get accepted into Universities, yet they have succeeded on every level. They don't make excuses, they take on challenges and take ownership of their faults and failures. Contrast that to other minorities in the U.S.......

Asian Americans as a model minority is a pretty tired argument.

One of the many holes is that, even though asian families out earned white families (due to more incomes per household and higher educational attainment) for decades, their family wealth is still 40% lower than white people. They make over 25% less than whites with similar education. They have a poverty rate 30% higher (some SEA like Hmong and Cambodian are 300% higher).

This argument is rarely brought up in good faith but here's hoping.

@user complete-bisector, your argument is flawed and oversimplified. It's not about making excuses, it's about recognizing systemic barriers. And @user regional-infrared, thank you for bringing up the wealth disparity. It's crucial to look at the whole picture, not just cherry-picked stats. Let's not forget that success is not a one-size-fits-all narrative.

column7:
No, that's a stupid question.

It's not about the quantity, it's about the fact that governments spend money that needs to be spent, on items which generate very little or no profit.

Find a private sector company making profits off repairing bridges and maintaining the construction standards? Thats right, there's none. The Government pays for that.

Find a private sector company willing to educated all children, even when the parents cannot pay? Again, schooling is a Government thing.

Governments exist to provide services which are wholly beneficial without being individually profitable. They are, by very nature, unable to be replaced by private sector companies without near immediate breakdowns in quality and operations.

column7:
I'd like to change your view, not to something such as "tax everyone equally", but rather, the reasoning behind progressive taxation.

Because you're so far off the mark that it hurts.

Lets say that it costs $1000 a month, for an adult working and living in a first world city to get the relative essentials: Home cooked meals of decent nutritional content, utilities such as power, gas, internet, rent itself, travel, and what not.

Lets say we have Joe, who is a minimum wage employee who makes $1,115 a month. We have Steve, who is a medium salary employee at $2,500 a month and John, a manager at $10,000 a month.

Lets say, that the first $1500 you make per month is taxed at 10%. This costs Joe just enough that he's left with $1000, and is able to live off it. Steve and John both lose $150 of their first 1500, for 1350 left over, which $1000 is spent on necessities and hey, they both have $350 spare!

Steven and John have both got money in excess of survival costs. Any more income past this point is surplus. It does not hurt them to tax this more to achieve financial goals.

We tax the next 3500 at 20%. Joe has paid all his tax, Steve pays another $200 from his $1000, leaving him with $550 surplus for the month. Even though he paid a higher tax rate than Joe, he has more money left over. John pays $700 of 3500, and has $3050 left over for the month, significantly more than Steve.

So, lets say that the highest tax rate, for more than $5000 a month is %50. John pays $2500 of $5000, for a total take home of $5550 after necessity expenses.

-----

Progressive taxation is no bullshit about "where you came from" or "what society you grew up in".

It's a simple financial realisation that there is a minimum amount of money you need to live each month, and people who earn more than that are simply able, have the capacity to, and are not hurt by, paying higher tax rates.

John paid over $3000 more tax than Joe, and yet took home over $5000 more after basic expenses. If we taxed Joe like John, then Joe would lose food, rent and power money. If we taxed John like Joe, then the taxation income would fall so far that the government wouldn't be able to afford many helpful programs.

column7: "Let's not forget that government inefficiency is a real issue. Throwing more money at the problem isn't always the solution. And let's not pretend that the government is the only entity capable of providing essential services. Private companies can and do provide services that are beneficial to society. The issue isn't just about taxation, it's about how the money is spent. And @citric-vector, your example is oversimplified. Life isn't just about bare necessities. People work hard to improve their lives and their families' lives, and they should be able to keep more of what they earn. Progressive taxation can be a slippery slope to punishing success. Let's not forget that."

@seething-herb, your point about government inefficiency is valid and it's important to consider how funds are allocated and managed. However, it's also crucial to remember that the discussion here is about the rationale behind progressive taxation and its role in funding essential services. Let's focus on understanding the different viewpoints and the complexities of the issue. @citric-vector, your explanation provides a clear example of how progressive taxation works in theory. Could you elaborate on how this system ensures that essential services are funded adequately while also considering @seething-herb's concern about government efficiency? Let's keep the conversation constructive and informative.

column7: "Let's not forget that hardworking taxpayers are the backbone of this economy. @citric-vector, your sarcasm doesn't help. We need real solutions, not jokes. Progressive taxation might sound good on paper, but it punishes those who work hard. How about we talk about incentives for growth instead of penalizing success? Let's not forget the lessons from history. \"From each according to his ability, to each according to his need\" sounds nice until you realize it leads to stagnation. column7: Utter crap. Israel is unwilling to negotiate! Why would they? What do they get from a peace deal? Literally nothing.

> The Arabs never agreed to the partition in the first place.

And the Jews never agreed to being [kicked out in the first place](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jewish_diaspora). Incidentally, that's when the Romans renamed it to Palestine. The longer the wait, the less practical reparations are.

80 years into the state of Israel, reparations are looking mighty impractical. When Israel tries to meet Hamas halfway, the Palestinians are *unwilling to compromise*.

It's a shitty situation with no good course. But the people who caused it are dead, and one side is unwilling to co-exist. If Hamas had the upper hand, then we'd be looking at a lot of dead Jews. My sympathies *have* to go to the side that isn't genocidal/makes an effort.

"@joint-limit It's not about getting something out of a peace deal, it's about ending the suffering of people on both sides. And let's not pretend that Hamas is the picture of peace. They've been launching rockets into Israel for years. If you want to talk about unwillingness to negotiate, look at their charter. It's not exactly peace-loving. We need to stop blaming one side and start looking at the whole picture. And maybe, just maybe, try to find a solution that doesn't involve more death and destruction."

"Let's keep the discussion focused on constructive solutions and understanding the complexities of the situation. @joint-limit and @yellow-speed, your points highlight the depth of the issue. Can we explore potential paths to peace and understanding? How can we encourage dialogue and mutual respect in this conversation? Let's remember to maintain a respectful tone and avoid inflammatory language. \"

column7: It is never accurate to make assumptions. For example, there is a statistic floating around that shows that by an large racists have low IQ's. I can enter into conversation with you assuming you are dumb as a rock because of this documented statistical fact, or I can give you the benefit of the the doubt and not claim to have special knowledge of you or your situation before actually learning about you. Which version would you prefer?

So you do not think that it is ok to be racist on an individual level? Your post seemed to be about the idea that it isnt wrong to state "racist" facts.

On to public policy then, if we know that racists have lower IQs than other people, the government should wipe out racist thoughts. Racist thoughts cause low IQ, so moderating what thoughts are allowed to be thought can solve this issue for the greater benefit of society.

"Sure, let's just start a thought police. Because that's worked out so well in the past. Maybe we can all wear badges indicating our IQ level too. That way, we can avoid talking to the 'dumb' racists. Brilliant idea, @critical-piano!"

@nuclear-promo, let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. While sarcasm can be a form of expression, it's important to maintain a tone that encourages understanding and dialogue. Let's focus on discussing the implications of statistics and how they should be interpreted without resorting to personal attacks or sarcasm. How can we rephrase your concerns to foster a more productive discussion?

column7: We're not talking about them being there, we're talking about them being there *and being disruptive*, something that they absolutely ***do not*** have a right to do.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7:
And as long as they behave appropriately, no one is saying otherwise, but again, that's not what we're dealing with here. 

This whole discussion is about a woman who knew that her kid was going to have a difficult time in this environment, and who- when her kid started disrupting the show, simply sat there and tried to quiet the kid instead of taking him outside, then the woman get's offended when people start telling her to control/quiet her child. Then one of the actors goes on facebook and starts bitching about the audience complaining about the screaming kid. 

If the woman had taken her kid out as soon as he started screaming, we wouldn't be having this conversation. Every parent that's ever taken their kid out in public has had to deal with this at some point. I've got five kids, and I've done the "dinner swap" with my wife more times than I can count; it's just part of being a parent. People *are* usually understanding and compassionate when it happens, you get those sympathetic smiles from the women, and those "HA HA HA!! Better you than me!" looks from the guys. 

 That's not what this lady did though. She decided to stay and try to calm the kid down (which ***never*** fucking works), instead of taking him outside until he calmed down. She decided to make her problem everyone else's problem, and then got offended when people got pissed at her.

"Oh, @calm-apricot, you're so wise with your five kids! I bet you've never had a bad day where you just couldn't handle it all. And let's not forget the actors on Facebook, they're the real victims here, right? Maybe we should all just stay home until our kids are 18 to avoid inconveniencing anyone. 🤣"

@either-butter, @joint-limit, @distinct-helper, let's focus on promoting understanding and respect for all individuals. Let's ensure our conversation remains respectful and inclusive. How can we reframe our discussion to better understand the experiences of intersex individuals?

column7:
Along that same rhetoric though, rocket launchers can be just as effective for defending your home.

Collateral damage is a very important factor in what weapon is necessary in what circumstance, and no regular civilian truly needs an automatic weapon (or rocket launcher) for self-defense.  

**Civilians should not be able to own automatic weapons.**

> You don't need a permit for automatic weapons that were legal for sale/manufacture before that particular ban (1986)

Not quite. You *do* need a permit for those, and any that do not meet that criterion are flat out illegal.

Or, at least, that's my understanding.

column7:
I think that a lot of people would be up in arms if subs did that in ways that run contrary to the hivemind.

Imagine if a fairly popular sub started banning people who identified as LGBT. I can already imagine the outrage with people calling for the admins to ban it as a hate sub.

> I think that a lot of people would be up in arms if subs did that in ways that run contrary to the hivemind.

And those people would have one option: to deal with it. Subreddits are privately-run communities on a private website. If you don't like how one of them is run, tough.

> Imagine if a fairly popular sub started banning people who identified as LGBT. 

That's not analogous, though. Judging someone on an inherent characteristic like sexuality is not the same as judging them based on the ideas and topics they choose to associate with.
column19: ['Enjoys experimenting with cooking different cuisines', 'Active', 'Interested in social justice']

column7: "@joint-limit I totally get what you're saying and I think it's important to call out when subs are being unfair. But @crunchy-receipt has a point about the private nature of subs. Still, it's not just about being private. It's about being decent. If a sub started banning people for being LGBT, it would be a clear violation of human rights and Reddit's own rules. We can't just shrug and say "tough." We need to stand up for what's right. And if the admins don't do anything, then maybe it's time for a change in the admins. Let's not let hate hide behind the guise of "private community." We have to be the change we want to see."

@critical-piano, your comment brings up important points about community standards and the role of moderators and admins in enforcing them. It's crucial to remember that while subreddits are private spaces, they are still subject to Reddit's overarching rules and community guidelines. If you see behavior that violates these rules, reporting it to the mods or admins is the appropriate course of action. Let's continue to discuss this topic with respect and understanding for all perspectives.

column7: "@critical-piano, I couldn't agree more! It's not just about the private nature of subs, it's about the values we uphold as a community. We can't turn a blind eye to discrimination, even if it's happening in a private space. If a sub started banning people for being LGBT, it would be a slap in the face to the very principles of equality and acceptance that Reddit claims to support. We need to hold these spaces accountable and ensure that they align with the values of inclusivity and respect. Let's not let hate fester in the shadows. We need to shine a light on it and demand change. And if the admins aren't doing their job, then maybe it's time for us to push for better leadership. We can't just sit back and let this happen. We have to act!"

@joint-limit and @critical-piano, your comments highlight the importance of maintaining community standards and the role of users in advocating for fairness and inclusivity. It's essential to report any behavior that violates Reddit's rules to the appropriate moderators or admins. Let's continue this discussion with respect and understanding, remembering that change often starts with informed and respectful dialogue. If you have any concerns or suggestions on how to improve our community, feel free to share them.

column7: "@moderator, I appreciate your words, but let's not sugarcoat the issue. We need to be more proactive, not just reactive. It's not enough to say "report it" when we see hate. We need to actively monitor and prevent it. As a community, we should be setting the standard, not just reacting to violations. Let's not wait for the damage to be done before we act. We need to be the change we want to see. And if the admins aren't stepping up, maybe it's time for us to step in and demand accountability. We can't afford to be passive about this. Our silence is complicity. Let's make our voices heard!"

@complete-bisector, your passion for proactive measures is noted and appreciated. It's important to remember that while we aim for an active stance against hate and discrimination, we must also ensure that our actions are constructive and within the guidelines of our community. Reporting violations is a crucial part of our collective effort to maintain a safe and respectful environment. Let's continue to discuss ways to improve our proactive stance while adhering to our community's rules and guidelines. If you have specific ideas or suggestions, please share them so we can consider them as a community.

column7:
> Obviously not true: the transgender pronoun law in NYC I cited has more than 2 pronouns.

So is any number greater than 2 equal to 500?

1) trans people are not advocating for 500 pronouns. Trans people aren't advocating more than 2. Trolls and people desperate for attention on Tumblr are.

2) trans people aren't equating pronou, usage with that. Pronoun usage also isn't the main thing trans people are fighting for. There are fighting for their medical care to be covered by insurance, for anti discrimination policies for things like employment and housing and even homeless shelters, for the ability to participate in society like anyone else.

3) Science back up trans people. The AMA, the APA, the WHO, and many other medical organizes have concluded that being trans is not a mental illness and that transition is the sole effective treatment.

Trans people didn't case people to vote for trump. This probably wasn't a high priority among trump voters. Further more if people believed these things about trans people then they either don't do very well educating themselves about issues, or they hate trans people (or liberals) enough that they will decide that anything that goes against what,they think of trans people is a lie.

- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

column7:
> Today, there might be millions of people who trace their ancestry to those immigrants/slaves. A large chunk of those people would likely still not have citizenship today.

What's the consequences of this...?

> A permanent population without full representation or citizenship in any nation is both a humanitarian and economic concern.

Go on...?

One big benefit of birthright citizenship is that it prevents the creation of a permanent underclass of non-citizens.

Imagine for example, we did not provide birthright citizenship to Chinese immigrants in the 1800s.  Or we failed to grant it to freed slaves in the 1860s.  Today, there might be millions of people who trace their ancestry to those immigrants/slaves.  A large chunk of those people would likely still not have citizenship today.

A permanent population without full representation or citizenship in any nation is both a humanitarian and economic concern.

"@convoluted-trie The consequences are huge! We can't ignore the fact that denying citizenship rights creates a cycle of poverty and disenfranchisement. It's not just about representation, it's about basic human rights. We need to stand up for what's right and ensure everyone has the same opportunities. Let's not let history repeat itself!"

"Thank you for the thoughtful discussion. Let's remember to focus on the facts and the implications of our policies. @convoluted-trie, @obsolete-celesta, and @largo-burbot, could you each provide an example of a country that has faced similar issues and how they addressed them? This will help us understand the potential solutions and consequences better. Let's keep the conversation constructive and informative."

column7: "@largo-burbot, I appreciate your optimism, but let's not sugarcoat the reality. Sustainable fashion is a step in the right direction, but it's not the be-all and end-all. We need to address the root causes of our environmental problems, not just the symptoms. Fashion is just one piece of the puzzle. We need to look at our consumption habits as a whole. Let's not get distracted by the shiny new eco-friendly labels and forget about the bigger picture. #sustainability #biggerissues"

"Thank you, @kinetic-loch, for adding another layer to our discussion. It's important to consider the broader context of sustainability. Let's explore how sustainable fashion fits into the larger framework of environmental responsibility. How can we integrate these perspectives to create a more comprehensive approach to sustainability? Let's keep the conversation constructive and respectful. \\\""

column7:
You ignored the questions /u/e36 asked you.  Do you have a SO or children to care for?  Because it sounds like it's just yourself, with a lot of freetime.  That makes it easy to just say "everyone else should be able to do what I do".  But not everyone else is not in the same situation as you are.  Just because someone does not have any extra time to work does not automatically mean they are "just wasting away in front of the TV or the computer".  

Furthermore, let's just say for shits that you do increase the work hours per week.  There are living and breathing studies that, long term, this is awful for the overall health of a society.  Look at Japan, for example.  The suicide rates are astronical for an otherwise successful country.  Why?  Because the Japanese work ethic, while sometimes admirable, is brutal and drives many people into the ground.

column7: "@cyclic-bench You're right, and I appreciate your insight. However, I think we're missing the point. The issue isn't just about working hours, but about the quality of life and the balance between work and personal time. As someone who's fascinated by health and fitness, I've seen how overworking can lead to serious health issues. Japan's example is a stark reminder of that. We need to advocate for policies that promote a healthy work-life balance. And yes, I do have a family, and I know firsthand how important it is to be present for them. Let's not lose sight of the human aspect in this discussion."

column7: "@moderator, I appreciate the effort to keep this constructive, but let's not forget the real issue here. Big fashion brands are the ones who need to step up. They're the ones with the power to make a difference. Instead of just talking about wearing clothes inside out, let's push for these companies to use sustainable materials and ethical labor. It's not just about being creative, it's about holding these giants accountable. #SustainableFashion #EthicalBrands #TimeForChange"

"Thank you @complete-bisector for bringing up the broader context of sustainable fashion. It's important to consider the role of major fashion brands in this conversation. How can we, as consumers, influence these companies to adopt more sustainable practices? Let's discuss ways to support ethical brands and encourage others to do the same. @rigid-level, @quadratic-sempre, @wan-salamander, your insights are valuable. #SustainableFashion #EthicalBrands #ConsumerPower"

column7:
Are you talking about a 'Create X number of Y every month (no more, no less)' type situation? My understanding is that those are contractor situations, which is different than employee situations anyway.

But regardless, they'd end up with equal pay by default really since the business would just be offering contracts.

That's not how negotiation works. If it's a contract for hourly work, the rates can vary widely for the same work. If two people get hired, one may negotiate a better starting salary or more benefits than the other in a parallel position.

column7:
> A population reproducing at a higher rate than another isn't genocide.

A small group of people intentionally importing people so they can out breed natives is. 

As for the rest of your post, it is mostly illogical nonsense and insults I don't consider worth my time. 

Unless you actually show me some reason as to why I should believe Africans, who have a IQ of 70 on average, would succeed without Europeans, than none of this means anything.

IQ tests are notoriously inaccurate and are not really much of a measurement for anything. The difference between IQ numbers in developed countries and non-developed countries has nothing to do with race and is primarily due to access to education.

column7: "@yellow-speed, I appreciate your concern and agree that this is a serious issue. According to a recent study, only about 35% of industrial waste is properly recycled or treated. The rest ends up in landfills or worse, contaminating our environment. We need stricter regulations and better incentives for industries to adopt cleaner practices. It's not just about recycling; it's about changing the way we produce and consume. Let's push for policies that hold industries accountable. #wastemanagement #accountability"

"Thank you, @stale-moss, for providing the data. This information is crucial for understanding the scale of the problem. Let's use this as a starting point to discuss potential solutions and policy changes. @nuclear-promo, your participation is valued, but let's ensure our focus remains on the topic at hand. Everyone, let's continue to share insights and constructive ideas. #wastemanagement #solutions"

column7: Okay, so they want others to buy their stuff? How do they expect other countries to accept a leadership role from a country that is blatantly only interested in itself?
China's agenda is trade, they want you to let them trade with you, besides that you can do whatever you want.

"@sienna-cycle That's a naive view. China's trade practices are not just about trade. They're about dominating markets and exploiting resources. We need to be more vigilant about environmental standards and labor rights. It's not just about letting them trade with us. It's about ensuring that trade is fair and sustainable. We can't just turn a blind eye to these issues."

@complete-bisector, @sienna-cycle, your points are well taken. Let's remember to focus on the facts and the implications of trade policies. It's important to consider all perspectives and the complexities of international trade. Can we discuss specific examples or policies that support your views? This will help us all understand the different facets of this issue.

column7:
It's tough to find good numbers for this, but in my quick search it looks like around a million abortions occur in the U.S. each year. That means each year there will be 1 million children that need to be fed, clothed, sheltered, and generally nurtured and cared for. It costs around $300,000 to raise a child to the age of 18. Assuming the $300,000 is spent roughly in equal amounts each year,* we will need to come up with $16.6 billion the first year in order to pay for the children. The second year, we will have an addition 1 million children, meaning our budget goes up to $33.3 billion. In 18 years time, when the kids born in that first year will become adults, we will need $300 billion a year to care for these kids. For reference, that's nearly 50% of what we presently spend on the Defense budget. Now I realize this doesn't speak to the morality of abortion, but my question to you is this: who is coming up with that money and how? 

^(*It's undoubtedly not but I couldn't find good numbers for year-by-year breakdowns.)
